sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 49, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The activated white box FOLLOWS the player! After LEFT, the white box moved from [35-39, 32-35] → [35-39, 28-31] → [35-39, 24-27], staying below the player. Player at [32-34, 24-27], white box at [35-39, 24-27]. Left box at [28-31, 16-19] still c.
- Plan: Move LEFT 2 more times to align with left box columns (16-19), then move UP so white box overlaps with left box to activate it.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 48 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The UP moves didn't change the player position! After 2 LEFT and 2 UP:
- Player still at [32-34, 16-19]
- White box still at [35-39, 16-19]
- Left box still at [28-31, 16-19], c
The UP moves didn't work! The player is at rows 32-34 and the left box is at rows 28-31. The player can't move UP because the left box is blocking the way (or there's some other obstacle).
Wait, let me reconsider. The player is at rows 32-34, cols 16-19. The left box is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. The player is directly below the left box. If the player tries to move UP, it would overlap with the left box, which might be blocked.
So the player can't move UP past the left box. I need a different approach.
Let me think about this differently. The white box is at [35-39, 16-19], directly below the player at [32-34, 16-19]. The left box is at [28-31, 16-19], directly above the player.
The white box is 4 rows tall (35-39) and the left box is 4 rows tall (28-31). The player is 3 rows tall (32-34).
If I could get the white box to overlap with the left box, that might activate it. But the white box follows the player, and the player can't move UP past the left box.
Wait, maybe I need to move the white box to the left box by moving the player in a different direction. What if I move the player RIGHT, then UP, then LEFT? That would move the white box right, then up, then left.
Actually, let me reconsider the game mechanics. Maybe the white box doesn't just follow the player - maybe it has its own behavior. Let me look at the history more carefully.
After the first activation (SPACE), the white box was at [32-35, 35-39] (to the right of the player).
After the second activation (SPACE), the white box was at [35-39, 32-35] (below the player).
So the white box's position relative to the player depends on where the player was when SPACE was pressed:
1. First activation: player was LEFT of the box, so white box stayed to the RIGHT
2. Second activation: player was ABOVE the box, so white box stayed BELOW
Wait, that doesn't quite work either. Let me re-examine:
- First activation: player at [29-31, 44-47], box at [24-27, 44-47]. Player BELOW box. After activation, white box at [24-27, 44-47] (same position as original box).
- Second activation: player at [32-35, 32-34], box at [32-35, 36-39]. Player LEFT of box. After activation, white box at [32-35, 35-39] (shifted left by 1).
- Third activation: player at [32-34, 32-35], box at [36-39, 32-35]. Player ABOVE box. After activation, white box at [35-39, 32-35] (shifted up by 1).
So the white box always stays at the original box position (with a 1-row/col shift toward the player). After activation, the white box follows the player, maintaining its relative position.
But wait, after the third activation, the white box was at [35-39, 32-35]. When the player moved LEFT, the white box also moved LEFT. When the player moved UP, the white box... didn't move (because the UP was blocked).
OK so the white box follows the player. The question is: how do I activate the left box?
Maybe I need to:
1. Move the player to a position where the white box can reach the left box
2. Or use SPACE in a different way
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the white box doesn't just follow the player - maybe it can be used to "paint" or "activate" other boxes when it overlaps with them.
The white box is at [35-39, 16-19] and the left box is at [28-31, 16-19]. They're in the same columns but different rows. The white box is below the left box, with the player in between.
What if I move the player DOWN? The white box would follow DOWN, moving away from the left box. That doesn't help.
What if I move the player RIGHT, then UP, then LEFT? The white box would follow, and if I can get the white box to overlap with the left box...
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The white box is 4x4 (rows 35-39, cols 16-19). The left box is 4x4 (rows 28-31, cols 16-19). They have the same size and columns.
If I could get the white box to rows 28-31, cols 16-19, it would overlap with the left box. To do that, I need to move the white box UP by 7 rows.
The white box follows the player, maintaining a fixed offset. Currently:
- Player: rows 32-34
- White box: rows 35-39
- Offset: white box is 1 row below the player's bottom
If I move the player UP, the white box would move UP too. But the player is blocked from moving UP by the left box.
Hmm, what if I move the player RIGHT first, then UP, then LEFT? Let's see:
1. RIGHT: player at [32-34, 20-23], white box at [35-39, 20-23]
2. UP: player at [29-31, 20-23], white box at [32-36, 20-23]
3. UP: player at [26-28, 20-23], white box at [29-33, 20-23]
4. LEFT: player at [26-28, 16-19], white box at [29-33, 16-19]
Now the white box is at rows 29-33, cols 16-19. The left box is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. They overlap at rows 29-31, cols 16-19!
But wait, when I try to move UP from rows 32-34, the player is at cols 16-19, and the left box is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. The player would overlap with the left box if it moves UP. So the UP is blocked.
But if I move RIGHT first, the player is at cols 20-23, and there's no box at rows 28-31, cols 20-23. So the player can move UP.
Let me try: RIGHT, UP, UP, LEFT. This should get the white box overlapping with the left box.
Actually wait, I need to be more careful. The player is 3 rows tall (3x4 shape from UP movement). Let me recalculate:
- Player at [32-34, 16-19] (3x4)
- RIGHT: player at [32-34, 20-23] (4x3, since RIGHT changes shape)
- Wait, no. The shape depends on the last movement direction. Let me re-examine.
From the history:
- After RIGHT: 4x3 shape
- After LEFT: 3x4 shape (since LEFT is horizontal... wait, that doesn't match)
Actually, let me re-examine. The player started at some position, and after various movements:
- After RIGHT: player shape 4x3
- After LEFT: player shape 3x4
- After UP: player shape... I need to check
From the current state, the player is at [32-34, 16-19] with shape 3x4. The last movement was UP (which didn't work), so the shape is still 3x4 from the previous LEFT movement.
Wait, but the UP didn't change the position, so the shape might not have changed either. Let me just try the sequence and see what happens.
Plan: RIGHT, UP, UP, LEFT to get the white box overlapping with the left box.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: UP moves blocked - player at [32-34, 16-19] directly below left box at [28-31, 16-19]. White box at [35-39, 16-19] follows player. Need to move around the left box to get white box overlapping it.
Plan: RIGHT to clear the column, then UP to move white box up, then LEFT to align with left box so white box overlaps it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at 32-34, 16-19 (3x4)
# White box at 35-39, 16-19
# Left box at 28-31, 16-19 (blocking UP)
# Strategy: RIGHT, UP, UP, LEFT to get white box overlapping left box

result = action(['RIGHT', 'UP', 'UP', 'LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After RIGHT, UP, UP, LEFT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'G', 'W', 'N'] and n['pixels'] >= 8:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], px={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
